name: Augeas
description: Augeas was a king of Elis in Greek mythology, son of Helios (the sun god) or Poseidon, depending on the source, and Naupidame. He was famed for his vast herds of cattle, which were kept in stables that had never been cleaned. Augeas's stables became legendary for their immense filth. His story is primarily remembered through the fifth labor of Heracles (Hercules), who was tasked by King Eurystheus to clean the Augean stables in a single day. Heracles accomplished this seemingly impossible feat by diverting the courses of the rivers Alpheus and Peneus to flow through the stables, washing away the accumulated dung. Augeas, however, refused to pay Heracles the agreed-upon reward, claiming that Heracles had been ordered to perform the labor by Eurystheus and thus owed him nothing. This led to a later conflict where Heracles returned, killed Augeas, and installed his son Phyleus as the new king of Elis. Augeas's story highlights themes of hubris, broken promises, and the ultimate triumph of justice and divine strength. The cleansing of the Augean stables serves as a metaphor for monumental tasks of purification and the power needed to overcome seemingly insurmountable obstacles. His legacy is primarily tied to the immense and disgusting filth of his stables and the ingenuity of Heracles in cleaning them.
subtitle: King of Elis and filthy stables

keyMyths.title: The Fifth Labor of Heracles
keyMyths.description: Heracles was tasked with cleaning Augeas's stables in a single day. He diverted two rivers to accomplish this feat, showcasing his strength and ingenuity.

keyMyths.title: Augeas's Refusal to Pay
keyMyths.description: Augeas refused to pay Heracles for cleaning the stables, leading to future conflict. This act of treachery resulted in his downfall.

keyMyths.title: Heracles' Revenge
keyMyths.description: Heracles later returned to Elis, killed Augeas, and installed Phyleus as king. This act avenged the broken promise and deceit.

sources.author: Apollodorus
